Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court

Party_Details.Name: Chen Songlin Michael

Facts.Text: On 21 October 2020, Mr Chen pleaded guilty to and was convicted of 13 offences under s 197 of the Companies Act 1967 for the failure of nine companies to lodge annual returns on time.
Mr Chen served as a nominee director in each of the Nominee Companies.
On 21 October 2020, Mr Chen pleaded guilty to and was convicted of 13 offences under s 197 of the Companies Act 1967, and he was fined.
Pursuant to his conviction, Mr Chen was automatically disqualified from acting as a director in any company under s 155 of the Companies Act 1967 for five years from 21 October 2020.
Mr Chen was then a director of each of the Eri entities.
Mr Chen, who was then a director of each of the Eri entities, ceased to be so.
Mr Chen holds 80 per cent of the shares of Eri Organisation Pte Ltd, with the other 20 per cent held by his wife.
Eri Organisation in turn wholly owns Eri Accounting Service Pte Ltd and Eri Secretarial Service Pte Ltd.
Mr Chen had also attended training courses on regulatory compliance.
The Eri entities have always been compliant with the regulatory requirements under the CA including the filing of annual returns prior to Mr Chen's disqualification in October 2020.

When he realised that the Nominee Companies had not filed their annual returns on time, Eri Secretarial sent to them and the other directors reminders urging them to remedy the non-compliance as soon as possible.
Mr Chen was unable to contact the foreign directors and shareholders of those companies.

Eri was also unable to strike off these companies from the register despite Mr Chen's efforts because of various difficulties, such as the restriction of Mr Chen's access to these companies' accounts by ACRA.
CEASY was incorporated in Singapore in October 2022.
Mr Chen is the majority shareholder of CEASY.
CEASY has three directors: Ms Bao Zehua, Ms Zhang Shuhui and Mr Chen Hao Rong.
Ms Bao was made a director of Eri Accounting and Ms Zhang was made a director of Eri Secretarial.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a person who is automatically disqualified under section 155(1) of the Companies Act 1967 due to persistent defaults in filing-related requirements should be granted court permission to act as a director of and/or take part in the management of a specific company.
Party_Details.Rule: Under section 155(1) of the Companies Act 1967, a person who has been persistently in default of statutory filing and notification requirements is automatically disqualified for five years from acting as a director, promoter, or taking part in the management of a company, unless the court grants permission. In considering whether to grant permission, courts balance the regulatory interest in protecting stakeholders and the public against the applicant's and company's interest in economically productive activity, evaluating factors such as: the applicant's capacity for future compliance; mitigating circumstances surrounding past non-compliance; the structure of the target company; and the necessity of the applicant's involvement.
Party_Details.Application: Michael Chen was automatically disqualified for five years starting 21 October 2020 due to persistent defaults in filing annual returns for nine nominee companies under his directorship. Prior to this disqualification, the Eri entities, which he majority-owned and directed, maintained a record of compliance. Following his disqualification, Chen ceased his directorships and subsequently attended regulatory compliance courses. He previously obtained court permission in 2021 (HC/OS 785/2021) to act as a director for the Eri entities. In October 2022, CEASY Tech Pte Ltd was incorporated, with Chen as the majority shareholder and three other individuals serving as directors. Facing alleged difficulties in securing banking facilities for CEASY—which Chen attributed to his disqualified status—he filed the present application seeking court permission to act as a director or take part in the management of CEASY. The legal tension centers on balancing the protective and punitive objectives of the statutory disqualification regime under section 155(1) of the Companies Act against the applicant's demonstrated rehabilitative steps, the nature of the target company, and the broader commercial necessity of his active managerial participation.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge identified that section 155(1) of the Companies Act 1967 imposes an automatic disqualification on persons who are persistently in default of filing and notification requirements, and that the statutory objective of this provision is predominantly protective. The judge distilled a non-exhaustive framework for section 155 applications, listing five factors: (a) the applicant's capacity for future compliance; (b) mitigating circumstances for past non-compliance; (c) the structure and nature of the company for which permission is sought; (d) the interests of the public, shareholders, creditors and other stakeholders and the risks to them; and (e) whether it is necessary for the applicant to be granted permission. Applying this framework, the judge evaluated Michael Chen's past conduct and found that, although his earlier defaults were serious, there were mitigating circumstances: he had caused reminders to be sent, attempted to contact foreign directors, and faced difficulties such as restricted access to company accounts that impeded striking off the companies. The judge relied on the fact that the Eri entities had historically complied with filing obligations, that Michael Chen had undertaken compliance training in 2021, and that after he obtained permission in HC/OS 785/2021, the Eri entities remained compliant. On the structure and nature of CEASY Tech Pte Ltd, the judge observed that it is an exempt private company limited by shares, and that Michael Chen's majority shareholding and influence over its directors meant that granting permission would allow him to be held directly accountable for compliance. Weighing all factors, including that Michael Chen had already served more than half of his five-year disqualification period and the absence of opposition from the Attorney-General, the judge held that the protective purpose of section 155 would not be undermined. The court accordingly allowed the application and granted the requested permission.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court

Party_Details.Name: Attorney-General

Facts.Text: In HC/OS 785/2021, the Minister for Finance, represented by the Attorney-General, did not oppose Michael Chen's application for permission to act as a director of and manage the Eri entities under section 155 of the Companies Act 1967.
The Minister did not object to Michael Chen's application for permission to act as a director of and/or manage CEASY Tech Pte Ltd under section 155 of the Companies Act 1967.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, in light of the statutory purpose of section 155(1) of the Companies Act 1967 and the applicant's history of defaults and subsequent conduct, the Attorney-General should oppose the applicant's request for court permission to act as a director of and manage the specified company.
Party_Details.Rule: When responding to an application under section 155(1) of the Companies Act 1967 for permission to act as a director or to take part in the management of a company, the Attorney-General may consider the statutory objective of the disqualification regime, which is to protect the public and corporate stakeholders from risks posed by individuals with a history of persistent defaults. Relevant considerations include the applicant's demonstrated capacity for future compliance, mitigating circumstances, the nature of the company, and the period of disqualification already served.
Party_Details.Application: The Attorney-General, acting on behalf of the Minister for Finance, serves as the respondent in applications seeking leave to bypass statutory directorship disqualifications under section 155(1) of the Companies Act. In this capacity, the Attorney-General assesses the applicant's prior compliance record, mitigating circumstances, rehabilitative efforts, and the potential risk to public and corporate stakeholders. Previously, the Attorney-General did not oppose Michael Chen's 2021 application (HC/OS 785/2021) to manage the Eri entities. In the present application (OA 561/2023), the Attorney-General reviewed Chen's subsequent compliance record, his completion of a significant portion of his five-year disqualification period, and the specific corporate structure of CEASY Tech Pte Ltd. The core legal and regulatory assessment involves determining whether permitting the applicant to manage this new entity undermines the protective intent of the disqualification regime or whether the applicant's recent conduct sufficiently mitigates any ongoing risk to the public and stakeholders.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge noted that the Attorney-General, representing the Minister for Finance, did not oppose Michael Chen's applications in either HC/OS 785/2021 or OA 561/2023. In the reasoning, the judge referred to the Attorney-General's acceptance that the factual background from HC/OS 785/2021 was relevant to the present application and to the Attorney-General's submissions that Michael Chen had demonstrated capacity for compliance since being granted permission to act as a director of the Eri entities. The judge also recorded the Attorney-General's position that Michael Chen's majority shareholding and influence over CEASY Tech Pte Ltd meant that granting permission would allow him to be held directly accountable for any future non-compliance, which was treated as a factor supporting the application. The absence of opposition from the Attorney-General was one of the contextual elements considered in the holistic assessment of whether the protective purpose of section 155(1) would be maintained while allowing Michael Chen to resume certain management functions.
